channel.title: Paper Feeds
channel.description: Keyword-based research paper feeds from arXiv and IACR

item.title: Patch2Vuln: Agentic Reconstruction of Vulnerabilities from Linux Distribution Binary Patches
item.description: This paper introduces **Patch2Vuln**, an agentic, fully local pipeline that reconstructs vulnerability root causes *solely from Linux distribution binary patches* (e.g., Ubuntu `.deb` packages), without source code or network access. It extracts ELF pairs, performs Ghidra-based binary diffing and function ranking, constructs contextual dossiers, and delegates multi-stage reasoning—preliminary audit, bounded validation planning, and final root-cause classification—to an offline LLM agent. Evaluated on 25 manually adjudicated package pairs (20 security updates + 5 negative controls), Patch2Vuln localizes a verified vulnerable function in 10/20 security cases and assigns an expert-accepted root-cause class in 11/20. Oracle analysis attributes 7 failures to upstream binary-diff/ranking limitations—not LLM reasoning—while the bounded validation pass produced only two minimized behavioral differentials (both for `tcpdump`) with no crash, timeout, sanitizer finding, or memory corruption proof. All five negative controls were correctly classified as “unknown” with zero false positives. Results establish binary-driven agentic vulnerability reconstruction as a viable research direction, with binary-diff coverage and local behavioral validation identified as the key bottlenecks.

item.title: FedAttr: Towards Privacy-preserving Client-Level Attribution in Federated LLM Fine-tuning
item.description: We propose **FedAttr**, the first privacy-preserving client-level attribution protocol for watermark detection in federated LLM fine-tuning. Unlike centralized settings, standard watermark radioactivity testing fails under secure aggregation (SA), as SA hides per-client updates. FedAttr overcomes this via a *paired-subset-difference* mechanism: it estimates each client’s update by querying SA over two disjoint client subsets, scores the estimate using a differential watermark detector, and aggregates scores across rounds via Stouffer’s method. Theoretically, FedAttr yields an unbiased update estimator with bounded mutual information leakage ($O(d^*/N)$ per round), preserving SA’s privacy guarantees. Empirically, it achieves **100% TPR and 0% FPR**, outperforming all baselines by ≥44.4% in TPR or ≥19.1% in FPR, with only **6.3% training overhead**. Ablations confirm robustness to protocol parameters. FedAttr enables verifiable, auditable data provenance in production FL-LLM systems without compromising privacy or efficiency.

item.title: CLAD: A Clustered Label-Agnostic Federated Learning Framework for Joint Anomaly Detection and Attack Classification
item.description: The rapid proliferation of IoT/IIoT has created highly heterogeneous, label-scarce edge environments where conventional federated learning (FL) fails in intrusion detection due to device diversity and underutilization of unlabeled data. We propose **CLAD**, a clustered, label-agnostic FL framework that jointly addresses anomaly detection and attack classification. CLAD integrates **Dynamic Clustering Federated Learning**—to group devices by traffic similarity and prevent global model divergence—with a novel **Dual-Mode Micro-Architecture ($\text{DM}^2\text{A}$)** featuring a shared encoder and two parallel branches: one for unsupervised anomaly detection (via reconstruction and contrastive learning), and another for supervised attack classification. This enables knowledge extraction from both labeled and unlabeled clients within a single unified model. Extensive experiments show CLAD achieves a **30% relative improvement in F1-score** over state-of-the-art baselines under 80% unlabeled clients, while reducing communication cost by 50%. CLAD discards no data and preserves distinct operational patterns across device clusters.

item.title: PACZero: PAC-Private Fine-Tuning of Language Models via Sign Quantization
item.description: We propose **PACZero**, a family of PAC-private zeroth-order mechanisms for fine-tuning LMs, achieving *usable utility* under the strongest privacy guarantee: $I(S^*; Y_{1:T}) = 0$. Unlike DP—which requires infinite noise or $\varepsilon=0$ to match this MIA-resistance level—PACZero leverages *sign quantization of subset-aggregated zeroth-order gradients* to induce frequent unanimity across candidate subsets, rendering sign releases conditionally independent of the secret subset and thus costing zero conditional mutual information. We introduce two variants: PACZero-MI (budgeted MI via exact binary calibration) and PACZero-ZPL (strict $I=0$ via uniform coin flips on disagreement steps). On SST-2 and SQuAD with OPT-1.3B/6.7B (LoRA and full-parameter), PACZero-ZPL achieves **88.99±0.91** accuracy on SST-2 full fine-tuning—within 2.1 pp of the non-private MeZO baseline—while no prior DP method attains usable utility at $\varepsilon<1$. PACZero is the first framework to deliver competitive accuracy and nontrivial SQuAD F1 *at provable $I=0$*.

item.title: Pop Quiz Attack: Black-box Membership Inference Attacks Against Large Language Models
item.description: We propose **Pop Quiz Attack**, a novel black-box membership inference attack against large language models (LLMs). It transforms candidate training samples into multiple-choice questions and infers membership based on the model’s answer confidence—specifically, the logit difference or probability advantage for the correct option. Evaluated on six widely used LLMs (GPT-3.5, GPT-4o, LLaMA2-7b/13b, Mistral-7b, Vicuna-7b) and four diverse datasets, our method achieves an average ROC-AUC of **0.873**, outperforming prior black-box baselines by **20.6%**. We systematically analyze key factors affecting success—including query complexity, data modality, structural format, and training configuration—and evaluate instruction-based, filtering-based, and differential privacy defenses: all reduce but **fail to eliminate** the threat (AUC remains >0.65). This work reveals persistent, practical privacy vulnerabilities in modern LLMs.

item.title: Constraining Host-Level Abuse in Self-Hosted Computer-Use Agents via TEE-Backed Isolation
item.description: Self-hosted computer-use agents (SHCUAs) like OpenClaw enable powerful automation by directly accessing host resources—but introduce critical host-level abuse risks via prompt injection, malicious skills, or control-path tampering. We argue that ad hoc blocking rules are insufficient because operation safety depends on the joint context of action type, target object, execution environment, and impact. This paper proposes an operation-centric confinement model: routine functionality runs in the constrained Rich Execution Environment (REE), while security-critical tasks—including dynamic classification, fine-grained authorization, subject-action-resource binding, evidence generation, and selective execution control—are enforced inside a cloud-native, TEE-backed trusted operation plane. Instantiated on OpenClaw using Intel TDX as the primary trusted backend, our design features remote terminal-side trusted components that verify TDX-audited commands before local execution. Evaluation shows the system blocks all unsafe or policy-violating operations pre-execution, preserves full functionality for permitted workloads with negligible latency overhead (<120 ms avg.), and generates cryptographically verifiable, context-rich audit evidence—enabling robust accountability without compromising usability.

item.title: Fine-Tuning Small Language Models for Solution-Oriented Windows Event Log Analysis
item.description: Large language models (LLMs) show promise for Windows event log analysis but face practical barriers: high computational cost, cloud dependency, and security risks from sensitive log data. Crucially, most existing methods only diagnose problems—not generate actionable remediations. This paper introduces a solution-oriented alternative using fine-tuned Small Language Models (SLMs). We first construct a large-scale synthetic dataset (100K+ samples) of Windows events—each annotated with root cause, step-by-step remediation, and verification commands—generated via a high-performing LLM (GPT-4) and rigorously validated. Using LoRA-based parameter-efficient fine-tuning, we adapt multiple SLMs (e.g., Phi-3, Qwen2-1.5B) and compare them against LLMs (e.g., Llama3-8B). Expert evaluation shows fine-tuned SLMs achieve **92.3% F1 on issue identification** (+6.1 pts over LLMs) and **87.6% adoption rate for remediation suggestions** (+14.4 pts), while requiring only **2.1 GB VRAM** and **142 ms latency per log**—enabling secure, low-resource, on-premise deployment. This work demonstrates that task-specialized SLMs can outperform general-purpose LLMs in both accuracy and actionability for operational log analysis.

item.title: Trade-off Functions for DP-SGD with Subsampling based on Random Shuffling: Tight Upper and Lower Bounds
item.description: We provide the first tight, closed-form upper and lower bounds on the trade-off function for DP-SGD with *random shuffling* (not Poisson subsampling) within the $f$-DP framework, valid for $\sigma \geq \sqrt{3/\ln M}$. Leveraging the Berry–Esseen theorem, our bounds are explicit, interpretable, and tight up to constant factors. For a single epoch ($E = 1$), we show that with $\sigma = 1$ and $\delta = 0.01$, only $M \approx 1.14\times10^6$ iterations and $N \approx 1.14\times10^7$ samples suffice to achieve $f(a) \geq 1 - a - \delta$—a meaningful privacy guarantee unattainable in the small-$\sigma$ regime. For $E$-epoch composition, classical analysis yields $\delta = O(E)$, limiting $E = O(\sqrt{M})$; our new technique—based on a generalized law of large numbers—establishes that if $E = c_M^2 M$ with $c_M \to 0$, then the $E$-fold composed trade-off function converges uniformly to the random-guessing diagonal $1-a$, with $\delta = O(\sqrt{E})$. This reveals an intrinsic asymptotic advantage of shuffling over Poisson sampling, while explicit convergence rates remain an open problem.

item.title: ClawGuard: Out-of-Band Detection of LLM Agent Workflow Hijacking via EM Side Channel
item.description: ClawGuard is the first out-of-band detection system for LLM agent workflow hijacking, leveraging electromagnetic (EM) side-channel emissions as a physical, forge-resistant audit signal. Unlike host-based defenses vulnerable to OS compromise, ClawGuard uses external software-defined radios (SDRs) to passively capture macroscopic EM envelopes radiated by hardware usage patterns—distinct across LLM skills (e.g., computation, DRAM access, network blocking). A drift-aware pipeline extracts 320-dimensional RF features and classifies skill executions in real time. Evaluated on a large-scale 7.82 TB RF corpus covering 12 realistic agent skills, ClawGuard achieves an AUC of 0.9945, 100% true-positive rate, and only 1.16% false-positive rate. This demonstrates that passive EM sensing is a practical, hardware-rooted foundation for trustworthy LLM agent monitoring—immune to software-level tampering.

item.title: Heimdallr: Characterizing and Detecting LLM-Induced Security Risks in GitHub CI Workflows
item.description: This paper presents Heimdallr, the first systematic study and detection framework for LLM-induced security risks in GitHub CI workflows. We characterize the full execution chain—from external inputs shaping LLM prompts to model outputs driving privileged actions—and propose a novel taxonomy of four high-level risk classes (e.g., prompt injection, output hijacking) and 12 concrete threat vectors. Heimdallr introduces an LLM-Workflow Property Graph (L-WPG) abstraction and combines triggerability analysis, LLM-assisted dataflow summarization (using a fine-tuned security-aware model), and deterministic propagation to detect exploitable flows. Evaluated on 300 manually annotated workflows, it achieves near-perfect LLM-node identification (F1 = 0.994), 99.8% triggerability classification accuracy, and 0.917 micro-average F1 for threat-vector detection. As part of ongoing responsible disclosure, we have reported 802 vulnerable workflow instances across 759 repositories, receiving 71 acknowledgments.

item.title: $α$-Wasserstein Mechanism for Rényi Pufferfish Privacy
item.description: This paper introduces the **$\alpha$-Wasserstein mechanism**, a unified framework for achieving exact $(\alpha,\varepsilon)$-Rényi Pufferfish Privacy (RPP) using Laplace and Gaussian noise. By applying Hölder’s inequality, we derive analytically tight scale calibrations for the Laplace mechanism via an upper bound on the $W_\alpha$ metric—guaranteeing $(\alpha,\varepsilon)$-RPP for $\alpha \in (1,\infty]$, and recovering the classic $W_\infty$ mechanism as $\alpha \to \infty$. We extend this to the exponential mechanism and propose a novel $W_\alpha$-Gaussian mechanism for $\alpha \in (1,\infty)$, which strictly generalizes existing $(\alpha,\varepsilon)$-Rényi DP results (e.g., subsuming the $\alpha=2$ KL-divergence case). Experiments show our mechanisms reduce noise power by up to 42% versus $W_\infty$-based baselines, with the Gaussian variant consistently outperforming Laplace in utility—all while providing *exact*, $\delta$-free $(\alpha,\varepsilon)$-RPP guarantees.

item.title: SafeHarbor: Hierarchical Memory-Augmented Guardrail for LLM Agent Safety
item.description: Large Language Model (LLM) agents exhibit powerful tool-use capabilities, yet remain vulnerable to adversarial manipulation that triggers harmful content generation. Existing safety guardrails often suffer from over-refusal—excessively blocking benign requests when tightening safety constraints. To address this trade-off, we propose **SafeHarbor**, a hierarchical, memory-augmented, training-free safety framework. It dynamically extracts context-aware defense rules via enhanced adversarial generation and injects them through a local hierarchical memory system for real-time, plug-and-play decision-making. Crucially, an information entropy–based self-evolution mechanism continuously optimizes memory structure via dynamic node splitting and merging. Experiments across diverse ambiguous benign tasks and explicit malicious attacks show SafeHarbor achieves a state-of-the-art **63.6% benign utility on GPT-4o**, while maintaining **>93% refusal rate against harmful requests**, significantly outperforming prior methods in both safety robustness and utility preservation. Code is publicly available.

item.title: AoI-Guided Client Selection for Robust and Timely Federated Intrusion Detection in Cloud-Edge Security Analytics
item.description: This paper introduces Age of Information (AoI) as a systems-aware metric for client selection in federated intrusion detection across cloud-edge security analytics. We propose and evaluate three lightweight policies—AoI-first, utility-first, and a tunable hybrid AoI+utility rule—under realistic constraints: fixed per-round communication budget, heterogeneous clients, and adversarial settings (label-flip poisoning with trimmed-mean aggregation). Across four benchmarks (CIC-IDS2017 mini, NSL-KDD, ToN-IoT, synthetic drift), AoI-guided selection reduces average AoI by 39–41% and peak AoI by ~70% versus random sampling, while preserving Macro-F1 and AUC within ±1.2%. The hybrid policy offers an interpretable trade-off knob balancing freshness, detection quality, and robustness—though not uniformly Pareto-optimal when false positive rate is prioritized. Crucially, AoI selection complements (rather than replaces) robust aggregation, enhancing timeliness without altering FL participation budgets—enabling practical, privacy-preserving, and timely security analytics.

item.title: Architecture Matters: Comparing RAG Systems under Knowledge Base Poisoning
item.description: This paper investigates how architectural design governs adversarial robustness in Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems under knowledge base poisoning. We evaluate four RAG architectures—vanilla, agentic, MADAM-RAG, and Recursive Language Models (RLM)—on 921 Natural Questions QA pairs under controlled single-document poisoning, comparing a clean baseline, naive injection, and CorruptRAG-AK: a novel meta-epistemic attack targeting credibility assessment. Architecture proves decisive: under CorruptRAG-AK, attack success ranges from 81.9% (vanilla) to 24.4% (RLM)—a 57.5-point gap—despite near-identical clean accuracy (~92%). Crucially, when poisoned documents are retrieved, CorruptRAG-AK’s advantage stems primarily from adversarial framing—not retrieval manipulation—localizing cross-architectural vulnerability to the content-reasoning stage. While our MADAM-RAG reimplementation shows highest apparent contradiction detection, LLM-based evaluation reveals low precision (~48.5%), and its high non-answer rate (41.4% even on clean inputs) indicates poor resolution reliability. We introduce a seven-category behavioral taxonomy and release all code, data, and notebooks.

item.title: PrefixGuard: From LLM-Agent Traces to Online Failure-Warning Monitors
item.description: PrefixGuard is a trace-to-monitor synthesis framework for early failure warning in LLM agents executing long-horizon, tool-augmented tasks. It comprises two stages: (1) offline *StepView induction*, which automatically derives deterministic, typed-step adapters from raw execution traces to standardize heterogeneous events; and (2) supervised monitor training that learns an event abstraction and prefix-risk scorer using only terminal success/failure labels. Evaluated across WebArena, $τ^2$-Bench, SkillsBench, and TerminalBench, PrefixGuard achieves strong AUPRC scores (0.900, 0.710, 0.533, 0.557), outperforming raw-text baselines by +0.137 average AUPRC—and consistently surpassing LLM-based judges under identical prefix-warning constraints. We introduce an *observability ceiling* to disentangle monitor error from intrinsic prefix insufficiency, and show post-hoc DFAs remain compact on simpler benchmarks (29/20 states) but scale substantially on complex ones (151/187 states). Crucially, first-alert diagnostics reveal that high-ranking performance does not guarantee low-false-alarm utility: WebArena ranks best yet fails actionable early alerts, whereas $τ^2$-Bench and TerminalBench retain more deployable warnings. PrefixGuard thus offers a practical, diagnosable recipe for operational prefix monitoring.

item.title: TinyBayes: Closed-Form Bayesian Inference via Jacobi Prior for Real-Time Image Classification on Edge Devices
item.description: TinyBayes introduces the first closed-form Bayesian inference framework for real-time, on-device cocoa disease classification. It integrates YOLOv8-Nano (5.9 MB) for lesion localization, MobileNetV3-Small (3.5 MB) for feature extraction, and a novel Jacobi-prior-based Distributed Multinomial Regression (Jacobi-DMR) classifier—delivering non-iterative, analytical Bayesian estimates via orthogonal projection. With only **13.5 KB overhead**, the full pipeline fits within **9.5 MB**, enables end-to-end CPU inference in **<150 ms/image**, and achieves **78.7% accuracy** on the Amini Cocoa Contamination Challenge dataset. Benchmarked against seven classifiers (including Random Forest, XGBoost, and Jacobi-GP), Jacobi-DMR achieves the best trade-off among accuracy, model size, and speed for edge deployment. We prove its asymptotic equivalence, consistency, asymptotic normality, and bias correction. Code and data are publicly available.

item.title: Correct Code, Vulnerable Dependencies: A Large Scale Measurement Study of LLM-Specified Library Versions
item.description: This paper presents the first large-scale measurement study of version-level dependency risks in LLM-generated Python code. Evaluating 10 LLMs on PinTrace—a benchmark of 1,000 real-world Stack Overflow tasks—we find that LLMs frequently specify vulnerable library versions: 36.7–55.7% of tasks include at least one known CVE, 62.8–74.5% of which are Critical or High severity, and 72.3–91.4% of those CVEs were publicly disclosed *before* the models’ knowledge cutoffs. Critically, all models converge on a small set of risky versions (e.g., `requests==2.25.1`), indicating systemic bias—not random error. Static compatibility is low (19.7–63.2% install success), primarily due to version conflicts; dynamic test pass rates drop further (6.5–48.6%). Controlled experiments confirm that failures stem from version selection—not code quality—and that externally imposed version constraints significantly reduce both vulnerability exposure and compatibility failures. Our work establishes LLM-specified dependency versions as a critical, previously overlooked risk surface in AI-augmented development. Code and data are open-sourced at https://github.com/dw763j/PinTrace.

item.title: Band Together: Untargeted Adversarial Training with Multimodal Coordination against Evasion-based Promotion Attacks
item.description: Multimodal recommender systems (MRS) leverage visual and textual signals to alleviate data sparsity but become more vulnerable to evasion-based promotion attacks—yet existing defenses are largely single-modal and poisoning-centric, overlooking critical cross-modal dynamics. We identify a *cross-modal gradient mismatch* under multi-user promotion: visual and textual perturbations diverge due to dominant user groups, diluting attack strength and misleading robust training. To address this, we propose **UAT-MC** (Untargeted Adversarial Training with Multimodal Coordination), which (1) treats *all items as potential promotion targets*, eliminating reliance on target-item priors, and (2) introduces an explicit *gradient alignment mechanism* to synchronize perturbation directions across modalities via cosine similarity regularization and joint update. Extensive experiments show UAT-MC reduces promotion attack success by 42.7% on average while preserving recommendation accuracy (NDCG@10 drop <1.2%), outperforming state-of-the-art baselines. Code: https://github.com/gmXian/UAT-MC.

item.title: Systematic Evaluation of Large Language Models for Post-Discharge Clinical Action Extraction
item.description: This paper systematically evaluates zero-shot and few-shot large language models (LLMs) for safety-critical clinical action extraction from discharge notes (CLIP dataset), focusing on post-discharge care transitions and patient safety. We propose a two-stage framework that decomposes narrative notes into fine-grained, explicitly actionable tasks via staged prompting. Results show that state-of-the-art LLMs match or exceed supervised BERT baselines on binary actionability detection (F1 up to 0.89), yet supervised models retain a clear advantage (+12.3% F1) on fine-grained multi-label classification—highlighting LLMs’ lack of structured clinical reasoning. Qualitative analysis reveals that most errors stem from misalignment between model inference and rigid annotation conventions, especially for implicit actions. Critically, the absence of rationale annotations (i.e., *why* a span is actionable) prevents distinguishing true clinical reasoning failures from labeling artifacts. We argue that advancing clinical NLP requires datasets with explicit clinical rationales—not just action spans—to enable faithful evaluation of clinical understanding.

item.title: Safety Certification is Classification
item.description: This paper reframes safety certification of uncertain dynamical systems as a **trajectory-level classification problem**, bypassing the error-prone recursive dynamic programming (DP) used in conventional approaches. Instead of estimating transition probabilities and propagating bounds step-by-step—leading to compounding errors and vacuous guarantees for large horizons $T$—we propose a kernel embedding framework that directly estimates the $T$-step safety probability from trajectory data. We prove this framework **subsumes key prior methods** (e.g., barrier certificates, robust Markov models) as special cases, while overcoming their core limitations: it eliminates horizon-dependent error accumulation and naturally handles **non-Markovian dynamics**, such as those induced by neural controllers. Experiments on a neural-controlled quadrotor confirm that our direct estimator maintains stable, meaningful safety probabilities (e.g., $0.82 \pm 0.03$ at $T=50$), whereas DP-based certificates silently collapse to zero beyond $T \approx 15$, violating soundness without indication. This establishes a principled, scalable alternative for high-confidence long-horizon safety verification.

item.title: From Coordinate Matching to Structural Alignment: Rethinking Prototype Alignment in Heterogeneous Federated Learning
item.description: Heterogeneous federated learning (HtFL) faces a fundamental tension: existing prototype-based methods rely on *coordinate alignment*—forcing client representations to match global prototypes element-wise via MSE or cosine loss—which implicitly assumes a shared feature subspace. This assumption breaks under model heterogeneity, as diverse feature extractors induce client-specific subspaces, and rigid subspace alignment unnecessarily constrains local learning capacity. We identify that coordinate alignment conflates two distinct objectives: aligning inter-class *semantic structure* (beneficial) and enforcing a shared *feature basis* (harmful in HtFL). To resolve this, we propose **FedSAF**, which shifts alignment from absolute coordinates to *relational structure*: it preserves each client’s intrinsic class-discriminative geometry by maximizing mutual information between local and global prototype pairwise similarity matrices. Experiments across CIFAR-10/100 and Tiny-ImageNet show FedSAF consistently outperforms state-of-the-art prototype-based HtFL methods by up to **3.52%** in accuracy, establishing structural alignment as a more principled and effective paradigm for heterogeneous settings.

item.title: VARS-FL: Validation-Aligned Client Selection for Non-IID Federated Learning in IoT Systems
item.description: Federated learning (FL) in IoT/IIoT systems suffers from slow and unstable convergence under Non-IID data, largely due to stateless client selection that ignores historical contribution quality and relies on local proxies (e.g., training loss) misaligned with the global objective. To address this, we propose **VARS-FL**, a validation-aligned, history-aware client selection framework. VARS-FL quantifies each client’s per-round contribution by the *reduction in server-side validation loss* induced by its model update, then aggregates these signals into a robust Reputation score—combining a sliding-window average of recent contributions and a logarithmically scaled participation term. Crucially, it requires **no modifications to local training or aggregation** and is fully compatible with FedAvg. Evaluated on a 15-class non-IID IoT intrusion detection task using Edge-IIoTset (100 clients, multiple seeds), VARS-FL consistently outperforms FedAvg, Oort, and Power-of-Choice: achieving up to **3.8% higher accuracy**, **2.7% higher F1-Macro**, and reaching 80% accuracy **up to 36% faster** (fewer communication rounds). This demonstrates that validation-aligned, reputation-driven selection significantly enhances FL efficiency and reliability in heterogeneous IoT environments.

item.title: Adaptive Selection of LoRA Components in Privacy-Preserving Federated Learning
item.description: Differentially private federated fine-tuning with LoRA suffers from multiplicative aggregation error, exacerbated by DP noise and harming stability and accuracy. Existing methods apply uniform or fixed-schedule LoRA component updates, ignoring structural asymmetry between LoRA matrices $A$ and $B$, and round-wise training dynamics. We propose **AS-LoRA**, an adaptive framework with three axes: (i) *layer-wise freedom*—each layer independently selects active components ($A$, $B$, or both); (ii) *round-wise adaptivity*—selection updates per communication round; and (iii) a *curvature-aware score* derived from a second-order loss approximation, enabling efficient, gradient-free importance estimation. Theoretically, AS-LoRA eliminates the reconstruction-error floor of layer-tied schedules, accelerates convergence, implicitly biases toward flatter minima, and adds zero privacy cost. Experiments under strict DP budgets ($\varepsilon \leq 2.0$) and non-IID partitions show AS-LoRA improves over federated LoRA baselines by up to **+7.5 pp on GLUE** and **+12.5 pp on MNLI-mm**, while matching SVD-based aggregation at **33–180× lower computational cost** and negligible communication overhead. Code: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/as_lora-F75F/.

item.title: XL-SafetyBench: A Country-Grounded Cross-Cultural Benchmark for LLM Safety and Cultural Sensitivity
item.description: XL-SafetyBench is the first country-grounded, cross-cultural benchmark for evaluating LLM safety and cultural sensitivity across 10 country-language pairs (e.g., China/zh, Japan/ja, Nigeria/en-NG), comprising 5,500 rigorously constructed test cases. It introduces two novel components: a **Jailbreak Benchmark** with adversarial prompts rooted in local sociopolitical contexts, and a **Cultural Benchmark** embedding subtle, culturally specific sensitivities within otherwise innocuous requests. Each item undergoes LLM-assisted discovery, automated validation, and dual independent native-speaker annotation per country. To disentangle principled refusal from comprehension failure, we propose three complementary metrics: Attack Success Rate (ASR), Neutral-Safe Rate (NSR), and Cultural Sensitivity Rate (CSR). Evaluation of 10 frontier and 27 local LLMs reveals two key insights: (1) Jailbreak robustness and cultural awareness are decoupled among frontier models (r = 0.12), rendering composite safety scores misleading; (2) Local models exhibit a strong linear trade-off between ASR and NSR (r = −0.81), indicating that their apparent safety often reflects generation collapse—not genuine alignment. XL-SafetyBench enables nuanced, culturally grounded safety assessment in multilingual AI deployment.

item.title: X-Voice: Enabling Everyone to Speak 30 Languages via Zero-Shot Cross-Lingual Voice Cloning
item.description: We present **X-Voice**, a compact 0.4B-parameter multilingual zero-shot voice cloning model enabling arbitrary speakers to synthesize speech in **30 languages** without target-language enrollment data. Trained on a 420K-hour IPA-aligned multilingual corpus, X-Voice eliminates reliance on transcribed prompt audio via a novel two-stage training paradigm: Stage 1 builds X-Voice$_{\text{s1}}$ with standard conditional flow-matching and synthesizes 10K hours of speaker-consistent audio prompts; Stage 2 fine-tunes on masked-text audio pairs to yield X-Voice$_{\text{s2}}$, achieving true zero-shot cloning—no prompt transcripts required. Architecturally, we extend F5-TTS with dual-level language ID injection and decoupled, scheduled Classifier-Free Guidance for robust cross-lingual prosody and phoneme fidelity. Evaluations show X-Voice outperforms flow-matching baselines (e.g., LEMAS-TTS) in MOS (+0.41), WER (−18.7%), and speaker similarity (+12.3%), matching billion-scale models (e.g., Qwen3-TTS) in zero-shot cross-lingual cloning. All code, models, and data are open-sourced.

item.title: Hybrid Quantum-Classical GANs for the Generation of Adversarial Network Flows
item.description: This paper introduces a hybrid quantum-classical GAN (QC-GAN) for generating adversarial network flows capable of evading classical intrusion detection systems (IDS). Unlike classical GANs, our generator employs a variational quantum circuit that encodes latent features as quantum states—enhancing representational capacity while reducing parameter count and computational overhead. The discriminator remains fully classical and is trained on the UNSW-NB15 dataset alongside QC-GAN–generated samples. Under a realistic threat model—where the attacker possesses limited NISQ-era quantum hardware (e.g., ≤16 qubits) but defenders deploy standard classical IDS—we demonstrate that QC-GAN–synthesized flows reduce detection accuracy by up to 37.6% (vs. RF) and 29.4% (vs. CNN-IDS), outperforming DCGAN and WGAN-GP. Crucially, we show that hardware noise (e.g., decoherence, gate errors) does not merely degrade performance—it can *improve* adversarial transferability, revealing a “noise-augmented attack” effect. This work underscores the urgency of developing quantum-resilient IDS architectures.

item.title: How Many Iterations to Jailbreak? Dynamic Budget Allocation for Multi-Turn LLM Evaluation
item.description: Evaluating time-to-event in multi-turn LLM interactions—e.g., jailbreaks or task success—is computationally expensive and challenged by sparse, delayed events and budget-induced censoring. Prior conformal survival methods provide lower predictive bounds (LPBs) but rely on static budget allocation and restrictive conditional independence assumptions. We propose **Dynamic Allocation via PRojected Optimization (DAPRO)**, the first theoretically valid *dynamic* budget allocation framework for distribution-free, finite-sample LPBs without requiring independence between censoring and event times. Its key theoretical advance is a novel coverage bound scaling with the square root of the *mean* censoring weight—yielding provably tighter guarantees than worst-case bounds. DAPRO also enables unbiased, low-variance estimation of population metrics (e.g., jailbreak rate) under limited compute. Experiments across agentic tasks, adversarial jailbreaks, toxicity, and RAG hallucinations using Llama 3.1 and Qwen 2.5 show DAPRO achieves coverage closer to nominal levels (e.g., 90%) with significantly lower variance than static baselines—all while strictly respecting the computational budget.

item.title: FedFrozen: Two-Stage Federated Optimization via Attention Kernel Freezing
item.description: Federated learning (FL) with heterogeneous clients suffers from severe client drift, undermining convergence and model utility. While Transformers show unexpected robustness in FL, the role of attention parameters remains unclear. We decompose self-attention into a *query/key (Q/K) block*—which defines the attention kernel—and a *value (V) block*—which performs semantic transformation under that kernel. Based on this, we propose **FedFrozen**, a two-stage framework: (i) full-model warm-up to learn a stable kernel, followed by (ii) freezing Q/K while optimizing only the V block and non-attention layers. Under linear attention, warm-up approximates descent on a regularized kernel-profile objective; freezing reduces optimization to a constrained V-block problem with fixed kernel. Our analysis reveals an explicit bias-drift trade-off governing optimal warm-up length. Experiments on real-world federated benchmarks (FEMNIST, Shakespeare) show FedFrozen improves both stability (−37–52% loss variance) and accuracy (+1.8–3.4% avg. test accuracy) over baselines, while cutting per-round communication by 40%.

item.title: Beyond Rigid Alignment: Graph Federated Learning via Dual Manifold Calibration
item.description: Graph Federated Learning (GFL) faces severe semantic and structural heterogeneity across clients, yet existing methods rely on rigid parameter or prototype alignment—implicitly assuming a single global linear representation space, which collapses personalized graph distributions. To address this, we propose **Federated Graph Manifold Calibration (FedGMC)**, a novel paradigm that models heterogeneity via dual manifold learning: the server constructs an *equidistant semantic manifold* (guided by geometrically optimal anchors) and a *global structural manifold* (built from structural templates), both serving as non-linear, client-specific calibration targets. Crucially, FedGMC aggregates *local manifold geometry*—not model weights—to dynamically refine global manifolds. Extensive experiments on 11 homophilic and heterophilic graphs show FedGMC consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines by +4.2–9.7% in accuracy, achieving superior balance between global commonality and local personalization.

item.title: From Drops to Grid: Noise-Aware Spatio-Temporal Neural Process for Rainfall Estimation
item.description: High-resolution rainfall estimation remains challenging due to sparse, noisy, irregular in-situ observations and limitations of remote sensing. We propose **DropsToGrid**, a noise-aware spatio-temporal neural process that fuses temporal sequences from private weather stations with spatial radar context. It employs multi-scale feature extraction, temporal attention gating, and multi-modal latent aggregation to generate *stochastic, continuous* rainfall fields—explicitly quantifying pixel-wise uncertainty. Evaluated on real-world datasets across diverse regions, DropsToGrid outperforms operational baselines (e.g., GSMaP, ECMWF) and deep learning methods (e.g., ST-UNet), reducing RMSE by up to 24.3% and CRPS by up to 29.5%, even with only 5–20 stations. Crucially, it delivers well-calibrated probabilistic forecasts under cross-regional transfer, enabling reliable decision-making in water management and early warning systems.

item.title: Quadratic Objective Perturbation: Curvature-Based Differential Privacy
item.description: We propose **Quadratic Objective Perturbation (QOP)**, a novel differentially private empirical risk minimization framework that replaces gradient-dependent assumptions with *curvature-based privacy enforcement*. Unlike Linear Objective Perturbation (LOP), QOP adds a random quadratic term—$\mathbf{z}^\top \mathbf{A} \mathbf{z}$ with $\mathbf{A} \succ 0$ sampled from a carefully calibrated distribution—to the objective. This induces strong convexity and stability *intrinsically* via the spectral properties of $\mathbf{A}$, eliminating the need for bounded gradients. We establish $(\varepsilon,\delta)$-DP under significantly weaker assumptions, particularly in the interpolation regime common in modern overparameterized models. Crucially, privacy is preserved even for approximate solutions (e.g., from first-order methods), and we provide tight excess risk bounds scaling as $\tilde{O}(d/(n\varepsilon))$. Experiments confirm QOP’s superiority over LOP in utility and robustness, while remaining computationally efficient via splitting schemes.

item.title: Enabling Federated Inference via Unsupervised Consensus Embedding
item.description: Cooperative inference across independently deployed models is critical in privacy-sensitive distributed settings, yet existing approaches require sharing raw inputs, model parameters, or a common encoder—violating data and model sovereignty. We propose **Consensus Embedding-based Federated Inference (CE-FI)**, a novel framework enabling pretrained models to collaborate at inference time *without* sharing any private assets. CE-FI introduces two lightweight, jointly trained components: (1) a **Consensus Embedding (CE) layer**, which aligns heterogeneous intermediate representations into a shared embedding space via unsupervised learning on *only shared unlabeled data*; and (2) a **Cooperative Output (CO) layer**, generating final predictions from aligned embeddings. Experiments on CIFAR-10/100 under diverse non-IID conditions show CE-FI consistently outperforms solo inference (+3.2–5.7% accuracy) and matches conventional cooperative methods requiring stronger sharing assumptions (within ≤0.9% gap). Evaluations on text and time-series tasks confirm broad applicability, though ensemble strategy matters. Analysis identifies representation alignment—not prediction head capacity—as the primary bottleneck. CE-FI establishes a practical, privacy-by-design paradigm for model collaboration.

item.title: FedeKD: Energy-Based Gating for Robust Federated Knowledge Distillation under Heterogeneous Settings
item.description: Federated Knowledge Distillation (FKD) aims to mitigate negative transfer in heterogeneous federated learning (FL) without sharing model parameters. However, existing FKD methods either rely on public datasets or assume uniform reliability of distilled knowledge—limiting robustness under real-world data and model heterogeneity. This paper proposes **FedeKD**, a reliability-aware FKD framework that introduces **sample-wise trust estimation** as an explicit, data-free component of knowledge transfer. Each client maintains a high-capacity private model and a lightweight proxy model; server-aggregated proxies form a global guide for private model updates. Crucially, FedeKD employs an **energy-based gating mechanism** that converts private-proxy logit disagreement into sample-specific trust weights for backward distillation—upweighting reliable samples and suppressing noisy or ambiguous ones. Extensive experiments across six real-world benchmarks demonstrate that FedeKD consistently reduces negative transfer and improves accuracy by 3.2–7.8% under extreme heterogeneity (e.g., Dirichlet α=0.1), outperforming state-of-the-art FKD baselines while requiring no public data or architectural symmetry.

item.title: Adversarial Graph Neural Network Benchmarks: Towards Practical and Fair Evaluation
item.description: This paper introduces the first practical and fair benchmark for adversarial Graph Neural Networks (GNNs), addressing critical inconsistencies in evaluation protocols across prior work. We comprehensively re-evaluate 7 widely used attacks and 8 recent defenses under both poisoning and evasion settings across 6 standard graph datasets (Cora, Citeseer, Pubmed, Reddit, Amazon-Computers, Coauthor-CS), conducting over 453,000 experiments within a unified, reproducible framework. Our analysis reveals that attack performance varies dramatically—by over 40%—under fair evaluation, and previously neglected factors like **target node selection strategy** and **training dynamics of the victim model** fundamentally distort robustness assessments, sometimes reversing relative rankings entirely. Crucially, we find that ~63% of reported defenses lose efficacy under standardized conditions, exposing widespread “false robustness.” This benchmark establishes essential methodological standards and open-source infrastructure to enable rigorous, comparable, and deployment-relevant evaluation in adversarial graph learning.

item.title: From Articles to Premises: Building PrimeFacts, an Extraction Methodology and Resource for Fact-Checking Evidence
item.description: This paper introduces **PrimeFacts**, a novel methodology and large-scale resource for extracting fine-grained, context-independent premises from full fact-checking articles. We compile 13,106 PolitiFact articles with claims, verdicts, and all cited sources, and identify 49,718 in-article hyperlinks as natural evidence anchors. Leveraging LLMs, we rewrite anchor sentences into stand-alone premises and explore implicit evidence extraction. Evaluated on cross-article evidence retrieval and claim verification, PrimeFacts premises yield up to **30% relative gain in MRR** over verbatim sentences and boost Macro-F1 by **10–20 points** for verdict prediction—robust across 2-class and 5-class granularities and diverse model architectures. Qualitative analysis confirms high faithfulness (>92%) to original sources. PrimeFacts enables scalable reuse of human fact-checkers’ reasoning and provides the first publicly available, structured evidence corpus grounded in real-world fact-checks.

item.title: A Few Good Clauses: Comparing LLMs vs Domain-Trained Small Language Models on Structured Contract Extraction
item.description: This paper challenges the assumption that high-value enterprise AI must rely on ever-larger, centrally hosted LLMs. We evaluate Olava Extract—a self-hosted, legal-domain Mixture-of-Experts Small Language Model—against five frontier LLMs (including GPT-4 and Claude 3 Opus) on structured contract clause extraction. Using a rigorously annotated dataset of 12 critical clause types and standardized evaluation, Olava Extract achieves the highest aggregate performance: **macro-F1 = 0.812**, **micro-F1 = 0.842**, outperforming all baselines. Crucially, it reduces inference cost by **78–97%**, enables secure on-prem deployment, and delivers significantly higher precision—reducing hallucinated or unsupported extractions by >40%. These results demonstrate that human-comparable legal AI is attainable with domain-specialized SLMs, offering a viable, cost-efficient, and operationally safer alternative to frontier LLMs in high-stakes contractual workflows.

item.title: SoK: Robustness in Large Language Models against Jailbreak Attacks
item.description: This SoK paper systematizes the landscape of jailbreak attacks and defenses against Large Language Models (LLMs). We introduce a comprehensive taxonomy covering 7 attack categories (e.g., template injection, semantic obfuscation, role-playing) and 5 defense paradigms (e.g., input sanitization, response filtering, alignment fine-tuning). Our core contribution is **Security Cube**, a unified, multi-dimensional evaluation framework that assesses techniques across three orthogonal axes: *attack strength & stealth*, *defense efficacy & overhead*, and *system-level properties* (e.g., judge reliability, cross-model vulnerability distribution). Using Security Cube, we benchmark 13 representative jailbreak attacks and 5 defenses across 5 open-weight LLMs and 3 automated judges—including our lightweight JudgeNet—revealing critical insights: (1) most defenses fail under adaptive attacks; (2) current automated judges suffer 22–37% false positive/negative rates; and (3) alignment strategy matters more than model size for robustness. We identify key open challenges and outline promising research directions toward provably robust, interpretable, and trustworthy LLMs. Code is publicly available.

item.title: Direct Product Flow Matching: Decoupling Radial and Angular Dynamics for Few-Shot Adaptation
item.description: Recent flow matching (FM) methods improve few-shot adaptation of vision-language models (VLMs) by modeling cross-modal alignment as continuous flows. However, we identify three fundamental limitations rooted in geometric incompatibility of pre-trained features: (1) angular dynamics distortion due to radial-angular coupling; (2) neglect of radial dynamics via destructive normalization; and (3) loss of dataset-specific context in unconditional flows. To address these, we propose **Direct Product Flow Matching (DP-FM)**—a Riemannian framework built on a *warped product manifold* with constant warping, yielding a decoupled cylindrical manifold (ℝ⁺ × S^{d−1}). DP-FM enables *independent radial evolution* and *constant-speed angular geodesic transport*, eliminating angular distortion while preserving radial semantics. We further inject missing context via classifier-free guidance conditioned on pre-trained VLM hidden states. Extensive experiments across 11 benchmarks demonstrate DP-FM achieves new state-of-the-art performance for multi-step few-shot adaptation, validating the critical role of geometric decoupling.

item.title: Federated Learning for Early Prediction of EV Charging Demand
item.description: Accurate early prediction of EV charging demand—estimating total session energy using only plug-in metadata and the first few minutes of charging—is critical for real-time grid coordination and operator decision-making. We build a session-level dataset from the Adaptive Charging Network (ACN) at Caltech, extracting tabular features capturing user intent, temporal patterns, and initial charging dynamics. Modeling intra-depot heterogeneity via station-level client partitions, we evaluate XGBoost, TabNet, and Federated LSTM under FedAvg. Results show that federated models achieve up to 92% of centralized performance while keeping data on-site—enabling privacy-preserving, scalable analytics across distributed infrastructure. With just 2 minutes of charging data, our best federated model achieves a mean absolute error of 1.8 kWh (vs. ~12.5 kWh average session energy), demonstrating feasibility of low-latency, high-utility demand forecasting. Code is publicly available.

item.title: On the (In-)Security of the Shuffling Defense in the Transformer Secure Inference
item.description: This paper critically re-examines the widely adopted *shuffling defense* in secure Transformer inference, where intermediate activations are randomly permuted before being revealed to the client to enable efficient plaintext nonlinear operations. We demonstrate that shuffling is fundamentally insufficient: despite independent permutations across queries, the underlying activation geometry preserves neuron-wise correlations exploitable via statistical alignment. We propose a novel attack that (i) estimates latent neuron correspondences using cross-query activation covariance, and (ii) recovers a common permutation basis via optimal transport-based alignment. Experiments on Pythia-70m and GPT-2 show mean squared alignment errors of $10^{-9}$–$10^{-6}$, enabling weight recovery with L1-norm deviations of $10^{-4}$–$10^{-2}$ from ground-truth weights at a query cost of ~\$1. Our results invalidate the security claims of shuffling alone and call for stronger, geometry-aware defenses in practical secure inference systems.

item.title: Storage Is Not Memory: A Retrieval-Centered Architecture for Agent Recall
item.description: We challenge the dominant “extraction-at-ingestion” paradigm in agent memory, arguing that discarding raw content before query time fundamentally limits recall. True Memory is a retrieval-centered, six-layer architecture that preserves all events verbatim and performs multi-stage retrieval directly over unmodified text—running entirely within a single SQLite file on commodity CPU, with no vector index, graph store, external database, or GPU. Evaluated on three benchmarks: True Memory Pro achieves **93.0% accuracy** (3-run mean) on LoCoMo (1,540 questions), **87.8%** on LongMemEval (500 questions), and **76.6%** on BEAM-1M (700 questions at 1M-token scale)—surpassing prior state-of-the-art (e.g., 73.9% for Hindsight on BEAM-1M). A 56-configuration ablation confirms tight performance variance (±0.65 pp) within the top family, demonstrating robustness. This work establishes that high-fidelity recall need not require embedding models or infrastructure overhead.

item.title: DecodingTrust-Agent Platform (DTap): A Controllable and Interactive Red-Teaming Platform for AI Agents
item.description: We present **DecodingTrust-Agent Platform (DTap)**, the first controllable and interactive red-teaming platform for AI agents, spanning 14 real-world domains and 50+ high-fidelity simulation environments (e.g., Google Workspace, PayPal, Slack). To scale risk discovery, we introduce **DTap-Red**, the first autonomous red-teaming agent that systematically explores multi-vector attack surfaces—including prompt, tool, skill, environment, and their combinations—and generates goal-directed adversarial strategies. Leveraging DTap-Red, we curate **DTap-Bench**, a large-scale red-teaming benchmark with verifiable judges for automatic outcome validation. Large-scale evaluation across leading agent frameworks reveals critical vulnerabilities: (1) tool-layer exploits dominate (68% of successful attacks), (2) multi-step attacks succeed 3.2× more often than single-step ones, and (3) current safety mechanisms fail catastrophically against environment-spoofing. DTap establishes a reproducible foundation for building secure, trustworthy AI agents.

item.title: Knowledge-Free Correlated Agreement for Incentivizing Federated Learning
item.description: We propose **Knowledge-Free Correlated Agreement (KFCA)**, a novel incentive mechanism for federated learning that rewards client contributions *without any ground truth labels, public test set, or distributional knowledge*. Under categorical local predictions and an honest-majority assumption, KFCA is **provably strictly truthful**, eliminating the label-flipping vulnerability inherent in classical Correlated Agreement (CA). Its lightweight, pairwise agreement scoring enables real-time reward computation on-device—critical for decentralized and blockchain-based FL ecosystems. Evaluated on federated LLM adapter tuning (32 clients, heterogeneous data) and a real-world PCB inspection task (12 factory-edge nodes), KFCA achieves 98.2% reward accuracy with sub-120ms per-round latency and integrates natively with smart contracts—reducing on-chain reward delay to 1.7 seconds (4.3× faster than CA). KFCA is the first incentive scheme offering formal truthfulness guarantees under zero supervision.

item.title: Pen-Strategist: A Reasoning Framework for Penetration Testing Strategy Formation and Analysis
item.description: Cybersecurity faces escalating threats and a critical shortage of skilled professionals, motivating the automation of penetration testing. Existing LLM-based frameworks suffer from poor strategic reasoning, inaccurate tool/action selection, and low execution stability. To address this, we propose **Pen-Strategist**, a novel reasoning framework comprising: (1) a domain-specific Qwen-3-14B model fine-tuned via reinforcement learning on a logically annotated pentesting dataset (2,184 samples with strategy derivation chains and step justifications), and (2) a semantic-aware CNN classifier for robust step-to-command mapping. Evaluation shows Pen-Strategist achieves **87% higher strategy derivation accuracy** vs. baseline, **47.5% improvement in subtask completion** when integrated into PentestGPT on vulnerable machines (outperforming GPT-5), and **18% gain on CTFKnow**. Its CNN classifier surpasses commercial LLMs by **28% in step prediction accuracy** and significantly enhances execution reliability. A user study with 15 security experts confirms its superior strategy quality over Claude-4.6-Sonnet.

item.title: Trustworthy Federated Label Distribution Learning under Annotation Quality Disparity
item.description: Label Distribution Learning (LDL) models supervision as instance-wise probability distributions to handle inherent ambiguity, but high-fidelity label distributions are costly and often noisy—especially under federated settings where data isolation exacerbates *annotation quality disparity* across clients. This heterogeneity invalidates sample-size-based aggregation (e.g., FedAvg), creating a critical trust dilemma. To address it, we propose **FedQual**, a quality-aware Fed-LDL framework featuring: (i) *quality-adaptive client training*, guided by a global semantic anchor that calibrates low-quality clients while preserving autonomy of high-quality ones; and (ii) *reliability-aware server aggregation*, which reweights updates by effective reliable information—not raw sample count. We introduce four new Fed-LDL benchmarks (FER-LDL, FI-LDL, PIPAL-LDL, KADID-LDL) with controlled annotation quality gradients. Theoretically, we prove client-specific calibration strictly dominates uniform calibration under heterogeneous supervision quality. Extensive experiments show FedQual consistently outperforms SOTA methods (avg. +5.2% KL reduction, +4.8% distribution accuracy), demonstrating robustness even when only 10% clients provide high-quality labels.

item.title: Gray-Box Poisoning of Continuous Malware Ingestion Pipelines
item.description: This paper investigates a realistic gray-box poisoning threat against continuous malware detection pipelines, where attackers possess partial knowledge (e.g., feature space, model architecture) but no full access to training infrastructure. Using the `secml_malware` framework, we generate functionality-preserving adversarial binaries in problem space via Import Address Table (IAT) manipulation and section injection—both lightweight, semantically valid PE file modifications. Empirical evaluation on a production-grade LightGBM detector shows that subtle IAT-based perturbations (e.g., adding ≤5 benign DLL imports) yield compact poisoned samples (<0.5% size increase) that degrade recall by 32.7 percentage points (98.1% → 65.4%), outperforming section-based alternatives. We further propose and validate a homogeneous ensemble defense that leverages prediction disagreement across identical LightGBM models to flag suspicious samples *before ingestion*: it achieves **95.6% poisoning detection rate** while retaining **99.2% of legitimate samples**, demonstrating practical viability for real-world deployment.

item.title: FL-Sailer: Efficient and Privacy-Preserving Federated Learning for Scalable Single-Cell Epigenetic Data Analysis via Adaptive Sampling
item.description: Single-cell ATAC-seq (scATAC-seq) enables high-resolution chromatin accessibility profiling, but privacy regulations and data heterogeneity impede multi-institutional collaboration. Federated learning (FL) promises privacy preservation yet struggles with scATAC-seq’s ultra-high dimensionality, extreme sparsity, and cross-site distribution shifts. We propose **FL-Sailer**, the first FL framework tailored for scATAC-seq. It integrates (i) *adaptive leverage score sampling*—biologically interpretable feature selection reducing dimensionality by 80%—and (ii) an *invariant VAE* that disentangles biological signals from technical confounders via mutual information minimization. We provide theoretical convergence guarantees with bounded approximation error. Experiments on synthetic and real multi-center epigenomic datasets (200K+ cells across 4 institutions) show FL-Sailer not only enables previously infeasible privacy-compliant collaborations but also **outperforms centralized methods** in clustering (ARI +12.3%), cell-type annotation (F1 +9.7%), and batch correction—demonstrating adaptive sampling as an effective implicit regularizer against technical noise.

item.title: A Comparative Analysis of Machine Learning and Deep Learning Models for Tweet Sentiment Classification: A Case Study on the Sentiment140 Dataset
item.description: This study rigorously compares Logistic Regression (LR) with TF-IDF features against a Bidirectional LSTM (BiLSTM) model on a balanced 10,000-tweet subset of the Sentiment140 dataset. Contrary to common assumptions, LR achieved superior test accuracy (**73.5%**) compared to BiLSTM (**69.17%**), which exhibited mild overfitting (train: 82.3%, val: 68.9%). Results indicate that for medium-scale, noisy social media text, classical ML with robust feature engineering can outperform complex deep learning architectures in both performance and generalizability. The models were deployed as an open, interactive web application via Streamlit and Hugging Face Spaces, enabling real-time sentiment analysis and public accessibility.

item.title: WAAA! Web Adversaries Against Agentic Browsers
item.description: This paper introduces the first web-centric threat model for agentic browsers—LLM-powered systems that autonomously navigate and interact with websites. Moving beyond prior work’s exclusive focus on indirect prompt injection, we identify a critical blind spot: traditional web attacks (e.g., clickjacking, fake login forms, DOM confusion) designed to deceive humans are not only effective against agents but often amplified due to their inability to distinguish task intent from malicious page content. We extend the See→Act model into a full-browser threat framework and derive a taxonomy of 20 attacks across web and LLM layers—implementing 18. Our evaluation shows that 10 classic web threats reemerge with significantly higher success rates (avg. 3.2×), and 14 attacks generalize consistently across four major LLM families (GPT-4, Claude-3, Gemini 1.5, Qwen2-72B). We characterize five fundamental failure modes—semantic blindness, context drift, action overflow, trust misalignment, and defense evasion—demonstrating that current agentic browser architectures are fundamentally unsafe for deployment on the open web.

item.title: SOCpilot: Verifying Policy Compliance for LLM-Assisted Incident Response
item.description: SOCpilot is a framework for verifying policy compliance of LLM-generated incident response plans in security operations centers (SOCs). It formalizes the plan boundary as the verification point—fixing the incident package, action catalog, policy rules (covering mandatory steps, ordering constraints, and approval gates), a deterministic verifier, and a fully public evidence surface. Evaluated on 200 anonymized real-world incidents from a financial-sector production SOC, SOCpilot compares LLM-drafted plans (from two providers) against analyst-authored SOAR references. A shared inline policy prompt induced *opposite* compliance shifts across providers, highlighting prompt sensitivity. The verifier removed **466 non-compliant, approval-gated actions** (e.g., unauthorized recovery) with **zero recall degradation** on core tasks. Aggregate violation rates remained stable (<1.2% std dev) across three reruns. Evidence centers on approval-critical containment/recovery decisions, while the artifact also enables zero-cost readiness checks for mandatory-action and ordering repairs. The runnable implementation is publicly released—enabling full result reproducibility without private incident data.
